claims.text: Mix it with baking soda at roughly two-to-one
claims.issue: The exact ratio for cream of tartar to baking soda can vary slightly depending on the acid strength and desired lift; 'roughly two-to-one' is in the right ballpark but not universally precise
Many modern baking powders are double-acting, but 'most' is difficult to verify without a comprehensive survey of current products

claims.text: The second is a slow one, usually sodium aluminum sulfate

script:
Two little tins in your cupboard, and the powders inside look identical — same fine white dust. But drop one in lemon juice and it erupts; the other just sits there, flat. Swap them in your cookies and you get sad, metallic, soapy discs. Today: why these twins do completely opposite things — and which one your recipe actually needs.

Here's what everyone believes: both make things rise, so they're interchangeable. They are not. And the difference comes down to one ingredient only one of them carries.

Start with baking soda. Its real name is sodium bicarbonate. On its own, in a dry tin, it does absolutely nothing. It's a base — the chemical opposite of an acid — just sitting there, patient, waiting for a partner.

Give it that partner and it wakes up instantly. Drop a spoonful into lemon juice and watch it erupt. That fizz is carbon dioxide, the exact same bubbles that lift your cake. But here's the catch: soda can only make that gas when it meets an acid. No acid, no bubbles.

So look at a recipe that calls for baking soda. Buttermilk. Yogurt. Brown sugar. Molasses. Cocoa powder. Notice anything? They're all a little acidic. That's not coincidence — that's the recipe quietly feeding the soda its partner.

And this is where your kitchen goes wrong. Use baking soda with no acid in the bowl, and most of it never reacts. It sits in the crumb, unspent — and unspent bicarbonate tastes bitter and soapy. That metallic tang on your tongue? Leftover base you never gave anything to neutralize.

Which raises the obvious question. If baking soda is helpless without an acid... how does baking powder rise all by itself, with nothing but water?

Because someone already solved the problem for you. Baking powder isn't a different chemical at all. Open it up, and the main ingredient is the very same sodium bicarbonate. They just premixed the acid in, in dry powder form, right in the tin.

The acid and the base sit side by side, harmless, because they're both bone dry. The instant liquid hits them, they dissolve, find each other, and react — no buttermilk required. Baking powder is a self-contained kit: base plus acid in one scoop, waiting for water.

The acid they usually use you might recognize from the spice aisle — cream of tartar, a powdery acid scraped out of wine barrels and purified. Mix it with baking soda at roughly two-to-one, add a pinch of cornstarch to keep it dry, and you've just made baking powder on your own counter. There's no secret ingredient. There never was.

But modern powder pulls off one more trick, and it's why your biscuits don't deflate before they reach the oven. Most tins say "double-acting" — two acids inside, set to fire at two different moments.

The first reacts the second liquid touches it: a little burst of bubbles in the bowl. The second is a slow one, usually sodium aluminum sulfate, and it stays asleep until the oven's heat wakes it — a bigger lift right as the batter sets. One rise to start, one to finish. That's why you can let batter sit a few minutes and it still puffs beautifully.

Now we can finally answer why a recipe picks one over the other — and it's the most useful thing here. It comes down to one question: is your batter already acidic?

If it is — a buttermilk pancake, a cocoa cake, a soda bread — the recipe uses plain baking soda. The batter brings its own acid, so you don't want the extra acid hiding inside powder. Bonus: the soda neutralizes that acidity, and a less acidic batter browns deeper and faster. That's the gorgeous dark crust on a good chocolate cookie.

But if your batter is neutral — a plain vanilla cake, a fluffy biscuit with nothing tangy in it — there's no acid for soda to grab. So the recipe reaches for powder, which carries its own. Now the leavening doesn't depend on what else is in the bowl.

And here's the part that quietly explains your worst disasters. Plenty of recipes call for both. That's not indecision: the soda reacts with an acidic ingredient and controls browning and flavor, while the powder guarantees enough lift on its own. Two different jobs, same bowl.

So when your cookies came out flat and soapy — you used soda where the recipe wanted powder, or you doubled the soda thinking more equals taller. But extra base with no extra acid doesn't rise more. It just leaves more unreacted bicarbonate behind, dragging the flavor bitter, and the gas it never made means no lift. Flat and soapy at once, from the same mistake.

The reverse has its own fingerprint: too much powder leaves a sharp, chemical bite — that's the slow aluminum acid, in excess.

So those two near-identical tins aren't rivals. One is the raw base, sharp and dependent, useful only when your recipe already brings the acid. The other is that same base wearing a backpack of acid, ready to work alone.

Which leaves one loose thread. If baking powder fires all on its own with just water — why do so many recipes still slip in a spoonful of plain soda anyway, even with no buttermilk in sight? The answer's hiding in the color of the crust. But that's a story about the Maillard reaction — and what really turns a cookie brown.
